blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
One of the most transformative shifts a ministry leader can make is from a needs-map to an assets-map — from seeing their community primarily as a collection of deficits that require external intervention to seeing it as a place rich with gifts, associations, and institutions that, when identified and mobilized, can generate genuine community transformation from the inside out. This session delivers that shift through the Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD) framework of John P. Kretzmann and John McKnight, contextualized through the DVULI lens of Christian community ministry.

Kretzmann's central claim is stark and bracing: 'Communities can only be built by focusing on the strengths and capacities of the citizens who call that community home.' This runs counter to virtually every dominant funding mechanism in the nonprofit sector, which rewards organizations for cataloging deficits — teen pregnancy rates, crime statistics, school dropout rates — rather than mapping the capacities already present. The ABCD framework identifies three concentric rings of community assets: Individual Assets (skills, knowledge, passions, and capacities of individual residents), Citizens' Associations (the local organizations — churches, civic clubs, athletic leagues, cultural groups — that aggregate individual gifts and multiply their impact), and Local Institutions (schools, libraries, police departments, hospitals, businesses) that, when reoriented toward community building, function as 'treasure chests' of resources available to the community.

The session grounds this framework in two vivid case studies. The first is Dr. Robert Lupton's Walker Avenue neighborhood in Atlanta — a portrait of a 'joyful entanglement' of relationships across race, class, and age that constitutes 'the Gospel in action' precisely because it refuses the anonymity and self-sufficiency that make community impossible. The second is Virginia Ward's experience mapping assets in her Cambridge, Massachusetts neighborhood — a practical account of the five-step Kretzmann-McKnight process (Mapping Community Assets → Building Relationships → Mobilizing for Economic Development → Convening the Community → Leveraging Outside Resources) that demonstrates ABCD as a ministry practice rather than an academic theory. The session concludes with participants planning and conducting their own neighborhood asset walk — an embodied exercise in community exegesis that will be debriefed in Session 11.
blocks.html:
Session 10

Community as Gift, Not Problem

Asset-Based Community Development: Seeing Your Neighborhood Through God's Eyes

Picture a ministry leader who spends three months preparing a grant proposal. She is good at this — she has done it many times. She pulls together the data: 38% of households below the poverty line, a 24% high school dropout rate, four reported gang-related incidents per week, a food desert stretching nine city blocks. She writes compellingly about the suffering she witnesses every day, and the proposal wins funding. She goes home and feels, strangely, exhausted and a little hollowed out. Something about the exercise troubles her, though she cannot quite name it.

What she has done is accurate. Every statistic is real. Every need is genuine. But John P. Kretzmann would say she has just spent three months looking at her neighbors as a collection of deficits — mapping what is broken, absent, and insufficient — rather than seeing the living community of gifted, resourceful people who call that neighborhood home. She has become, without intending to, a professional cartographer of suffering. And in doing so, she has subtly reinforced the very disempowerment she hopes to undo. This session invites you into a different kind of mapmaking entirely.

The Tyranny of the Needs Map

In their landmark 1993 study, Building Communities from the Inside Out, John P. Kretzmann and John L. McKnight identified what they called the "near monopoly power of the Needs Map" in American urban ministry and community development. They observed that virtually every dominant funding mechanism in the nonprofit sector rewards organizations for cataloging deficits — teen pregnancy rates, crime statistics, school dropout rates, unemployment figures. Drawing a compelling Needs Map, Kretzmann argued, "is the key to opening the vaults of most government programs, and most other funders as well" (Kretzmann & McKnight, 1993).

The consequences of this funding logic are profound and largely invisible. When a community repeatedly tells its own story through the language of deficiency, that narrative shapes how residents come to see themselves — as people with problems to be solved by outside experts, rather than as people with gifts to be mobilized for community transformation. The church or ministry that adopts this framework, even with the best of intentions, can inadvertently become what Cormac Russell (2018) calls a "service-led" rather than "citizen-led" operation — one that positions itself as the provider of solutions to a community of passive recipients.

This is precisely the critique Robert D. Lupton pressed for decades through his work at FCS Urban Ministries in Atlanta. In Toxic Charity (2011), Lupton documented how well-intentioned charitable giving and service programs routinely undermine community dignity, create dependency, and displace the local solutions that communities are more than capable of generating themselves. "When we do for others what they can do for themselves," Lupton wrote with characteristic directness, "we disempower them" (Lupton, 2011). The Needs Map, for all its accuracy, is spiritually and practically corrosive because it denies the Imago Dei in the people it describes — the truth that every human being, made in God's image, is a bearer of gifts, not merely a carrier of needs.

Think About It

Before reading further, pause and consider: If someone mapped your neighborhood exclusively through its deficits — unemployment, health disparities, crime, dropout rates — what would they miss? What living reality, what richness of relationship and skill and faith, would remain entirely invisible on that map?

The Assets Map: A Paradigm Shift

The alternative Kretzmann and McKnight proposed is not naive optimism. It does not pretend that poverty, violence, and structural injustice are not real. It simply insists that communities can only be rebuilt from the inside out — and that the inside of any community, however distressed, contains vastly more than any Needs Map reveals. "Communities can only be built by focusing on the strengths and capacities of the citizens who call that community home," Kretzmann wrote in his widely read 1995 article distilling the framework (Kretzmann, 1995). This is the foundational claim of Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD).

What does an Assets Map reveal? It begins with a different question: not "What is wrong here?" but "What is present here?" It asks what gifts, skills, passions, associations, institutions, and connections already exist within the community that — when identified, connected, and mobilized — can serve as the raw material of transformation. McKnight and Block (2010) extended this logic into a broader philosophical claim in The Abundant Community, arguing that neighborhoods already possess sufficient gifts, capacities, and associations to meet their own needs when those assets are identified and connected. The problem is not a scarcity of resources; it is that the consumer culture has trained people to look outward for solutions, systematically undermining what McKnight and Block call "neighborhood competence."

This is not merely a strategic insight — it is a theological one. The DVULI framework has consistently grounded community work in the recognition that God has gone before us into every neighborhood. As the "Entering the Community" reading in your Second Local Workshop noted, "Various authors have emphasized the fact that God has gone before us in our communities. Oftentimes Christians enter urban communities at top speed, full of arrogance and zeal... We lack the humility of Christ, so impatient to develop our own vision that we fail to see God quietly at work" (TelChar Associates, 2014). Asset mapping, rightly understood, is an act of theological discernment — learning to see what God has already placed in the neighborhood before we arrived.

The Three Rings of Community Assets

The Kretzmann-McKnight model identifies three concentric rings of community assets, each building outward from the most personal to the most institutional. Understanding these rings is essential preparation for the neighborhood asset walk you will conduct before Session 11.

Ring One: Individual Assets

At the center of every community's assets map lie the gifts of individual residents — their knowledge, skills, passions, experiences, and willingness to be involved. Kretzmann and McKnight were insistent on a principle that, stated plainly, is almost self-evident but is systematically violated by deficit-based approaches: every person in this community is gifted, and every person in this community will contribute their gifts and resources (Kretzmann & McKnight, 1993). This is the "most important" commitment for community building because it refuses the categorization of some residents as burdens rather than contributors.

This principle is especially vital for those typically defined exclusively by their needs: the elderly, the young, the disabled, those experiencing poverty. John McKnight (2013) applied ABCD principles specifically to people with cognitive disabilities, arguing that communities are impoverished when they fail to recognize and incorporate marginalized members' contributions. From a Christian perspective, this resonates powerfully with the teaching of 1 Peter 4:10-11 — the basis for the devotion that opened your First Local Workshop — which insists that every believer has at least one gift and that non-use is not an option. To conduct an asset inventory is to act on that theological conviction at a neighborhood scale.

Ring Two: Citizens' Associations

The second ring comprises what Kretzmann and McKnight call "citizens' associations" — the locally organized groups in which residents come together to pursue shared interests and activities. These include churches, sports leagues, cultural clubs, block associations, civic organizations, ethnic associations, parent groups, neighborhood watch programs, and countless informal networks. Kretzmann (1995) noted that community mobilizers from de Tocqueville to Martin Luther King Jr. to Saul Alinsky recognized these associations as "the bedrock for organizing."

A critical insight of the ABCD research is that these associations are far more numerous and more willing to take on community-building tasks than most leaders expect. One low-income Chicago neighborhood, when asked to conduct an associational inventory, identified 249 local associations — a number that astonished its own residents (Kretzmann & McKnight, 1993). The concept of social capital developed by Nan Lin (2001) provides academic grounding for this observation: it is through associations and networks that resources — information, trust, access, influence — are embedded and mobilized. Where associations are strong, communities are resilient; where they atrophy, vulnerability deepens.

Ring Three: Local Institutions

The outermost ring consists of the formal institutions physically located within the community: schools, libraries, parks, police departments, hospitals, community colleges, and local businesses. Kretzmann and McKnight described these institutions as "treasure chests" of potential community-building resources. A school, for example, contains facilities and space that could host community groups, computers and equipment that could serve local enterprises, teachers who could bring expertise to bear on community issues, and young people who "could come back into the community as contributors to the rebuilding process" (Kretzmann, 1995).

The key distinction is whether these institutions are oriented toward the community or merely located in it. When a school functions only as a service provider to its enrolled students and then locks its doors at 3:00 pm, it is not functioning as a community asset. When it opens its gym for evening athletics programs, hosts neighborhood association meetings, and hires locally, it is. Xavier de Souza Briggs (2004) at Harvard's Kennedy School found that asset mobilization is most effective when institutions are genuinely reoriented toward community building — while cautioning that the structural and political economic conditions that shape institutional behavior cannot be ignored. Transformation of institutions requires relational investment and sustained advocacy, not just mapping.

Walker Avenue: A Portrait of Joyful Entanglement

The most powerful illustrations of ABCD in the DVULI curriculum come not from academic theory but from lived practice. One of the most memorable is Robert Lupton's account of Walker Avenue, the Atlanta neighborhood where FCS Urban Ministries had been at work for years. Lupton described the bimonthly Tapestry Community meeting held at LaVerne's home — "a small house built by volunteers from an Atlanta church" — as a microcosm of what genuine community looks like: "young and elderly, black and white, rich and poor, homeowners all" (Lupton, 1997, as reprinted in the DVULI Second Local Workshop Pre-Assignment).

What is striking about Lupton's account is not the absence of problems — there were plenty of those — but the nature of the community's response to them. A stolen motorcycle was recovered by neighbors, not by police. Men volunteered to be "minutemen" for single mothers. An elderly neighbor named Mr. Boyd offered fifty dollars of his "beer money" for playground sand. The meeting was simultaneously mundane and sacred: lawn mower policies discussed alongside women's Bible studies, park workdays alongside the May elections.

"Night falls. But the darkness does not bring fear. Terror that stalks nearby city streets does not come here tonight. There is safety in belonging. Neighbors have made commitments to each other... In this one small haven in a storm of urban trouble there is peace. It is called Community. And it is the Gospel in action." — Robert D. Lupton (1997)

What Lupton describes is, in ABCD terms, all three rings fully activated: individual gifts being offered (Mr. Boyd's fifty dollars, the men's volunteerism, LaVerne's hospitality), associations functioning (the Tapestry Community meeting, the women's Bible study, the neighborhood watch), and the institutional resources of the neighborhood being claimed by its residents. Lupton was clear-eyed about the mess of this — the conflicts not voiced at the meeting, the jealousy, the resentment, the lawn mower conflict. But he insisted this "joyful entanglement" of relationships across race, class, and age constitutes "the Gospel in action precisely because it refuses the anonymity and self-sufficiency that make community impossible" (Lupton, 1997).

Steve Corbett and Brian Fikkert (2009) in When Helping Hurts offered a complementary theological framework, arguing that poverty at its deepest level is not a scarcity of material resources but a breakdown in relationships — with God, with self, with others, and with creation. The ABCD approach matters theologically because it refuses to treat community members as isolated individuals defined by their economic circumstances, and instead restores the relational fabric that genuine human flourishing requires. Restoration of community, in this view, is not merely a social strategy; it is participation in the redemptive work of God.

Virginia Ward's Five Steps: ABCD as Ministry Practice

For DVULI participants, the most practically grounding account of ABCD in action comes from Virginia Ward, a DVULI graduate and facilitator who mapped assets in her Cambridge, Massachusetts neighborhood while serving as youth pastor at Abundant Life Church. Ward's account, published in Network Magazine (2007) and used extensively in the Second Local Workshop, is notable for two reasons: it shows ABCD working in a real ministry context, and it demonstrates how humbling and time-consuming the process actually is — her community asset map took fifteen months to complete.

Ward followed the five-step process articulated by Kretzmann and McKnight in Building Communities from the Inside Out:

  1. Mapping Community Assets — Gathering written information about the community (census data, neighborhood surveys, police reports) and constructing both a needs map and an assets map as baseline tools.
  2. Building Relationships — Identifying key stakeholders (not obvious elected officials, but "the elderly woman who sits on her porch, seeing everything and everybody") and developing genuine, sustained relationships with them through consistent engagement over issues that matter to the community.
  3. Mobilizing for Economic Development and Information Sharing — Using the asset map as a foundation for connecting individuals and organizations to one another, creating economic opportunities rooted in existing community capacities.
  4. Convening the Community to Develop a Vision and a Plan — Bringing together the community's diverse stakeholders to articulate a shared vision for neighborhood transformation that is genuinely owned from the inside out.
  5. Leveraging Outside Resources to Support Locally Driven Development — Only after an internal vision and plan are established do you turn to outside resources — and now you can do so from a position of power rather than dependency, saying as Kretzmann (1995) put it: "We are mobilized and powerful; we are a sure-fire investment."

What makes Ward's account especially instructive is what happened when her church stopped cataloging problems and started mapping assets. They discovered that their neighbors shared many of the same concerns for the neighborhood's youth — a discovery that would have been invisible on any Needs Map. This discovery led to the collaborative "Theatre of Entrepreneurs" project, which united the community agencies of Riverside for the well-being of youth. "Relationships with the stakeholders are built with consistent engagement over issues that concern the community," Ward wrote, "not just the interest of the church's goal of evangelism" (Ward, 2007).

Think About It

In Ward's account, the asset-mapping process took fifteen months. Most ministry programs are planned on twelve-month grant cycles. What would it mean for your ministry's approach to community if you committed to a fifteen-month listening and mapping process before launching any new initiative? What would you need to let go of?

The Community Asset Inventory Builder

The following interactive tool mirrors the three-ring Kretzmann-McKnight model, allowing you to begin building your own community asset inventory before conducting the neighborhood asset walk assigned for this session. This is the same kind of "Capacity Inventory" Kretzmann described — "a questionnaire aimed at uncovering a person's skills, areas of knowledge and experience, commitments, and willingness to be involved in community building" — applied across all three rings (Kretzmann, 1995).

🔄 Interactive: Community Asset Inventory Builder

Work through all three rings. When complete, click "Generate Capacity Profile" to see your community's asset landscape and highest-leverage partnership opportunities.

INNERMOST RING — Individual Gifts & Capacities

Identify up to five individuals in your community. Map their gifts of the head (knowledge), hand (skills), and heart (passions). These are the residents Kretzmann says are "too old, or too young, or too disabled, or too poor to have any gifts" — who are almost never asked to contribute.

MIDDLE RING — Citizens' Associations

Check which association types exist in your neighborhood. For those present, note a known leader's name. Remember: one Chicago neighborhood mapped 249 local associations nobody expected to find.

OUTER RING — Local Institutions

Rate each institution's community-orientation on a 1–5 scale. 1 = purely extractive (takes from community), 5 = actively community-building (functions as a "treasure chest" of shared resources).

Betterment vs. Development: The Critical Distinction

Embedded within the ABCD framework is what the DVULI Second Local Workshop identified as one of the most practically challenging distinctions in all of community ministry: the difference between betterment and development. This distinction is not about whether activities are good or bad; virtually all betterment programs are well-intentioned and genuinely helpful in the short term. It is a question of orientation, ownership, and long-term impact.

The DVULI Workbook articulated the spectrum this way:

  • Individual Betterment (Charity): Skills and activities that help overcome obstacles to self-worth and personal development — doing things for individuals.
  • Individual Development: Skills and activities that foster capacity and sustainable personal growth — doing things with individuals to build their own agency.
  • Community Betterment (Advocacy): Resources and activities that mitigate or rectify community problems — addressing symptoms on behalf of a community.
  • Community Development (Transformation): Resources accessed and activities engaged by residents themselves that build and lead to positive change and well-being — communities transforming themselves from the inside out.

Lupton (2011) described a similar continuum running from relief (addressing immediate crisis needs) through rehabilitation (restoring pre-crisis capacity) to development (enabling people to sustain themselves and their communities). He was insistent that well-intentioned churches and ministries routinely apply relief strategies to situations that call for development, with damaging results. A food pantry that never connects recipients to employment training, job networking, or financial literacy may be genuinely meeting an immediate need while inadvertently deepening the dependency it intended to alleviate.

This is not an argument against charity or relief. As Corbett and Fikkert (2009) carefully distinguished, there are genuine crisis situations — acute poverty, disaster, emergency — where relief is not only appropriate but required. The diagnostic question that ABCD insists upon is: What does this particular situation actually require? Is the person or community in a crisis that requires immediate provision from the outside, in a rehabilitation phase where capacity is being restored, or in a position where development — working alongside residents as full partners — is what the moment demands? Failing to make this diagnosis, and defaulting to relief or betterment when development is called for, is what Corbett and Fikkert (2009) call a leading cause of "hurting the poor."

"Development is about building community muscle. It is about reducing the need for external subsidies. It's about harnessing economic forces that can pull a community out of the mire of dependency and onto an economically viable playing field." — Robert D. Lupton (1997)

The shift from betterment to development is not primarily a programmatic shift — it is a perceptual and relational one. It begins when a ministry leader stops asking "What does this community need from us?" and starts asking "What does this community already have, and how can we come alongside as partners rather than providers?" Karen Pittman, quoted in the DVULI Second Local Workshop Workbook, captured the essential move: "Community Youth Development helps us move from seeing young people as problems to seeing young people as problem-solvers and leaders. Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged."

Community Exegesis: Reading the Story of Your Neighborhood

One of the most powerful practices in the DVULI Second Local Workshop was what Michael Mata, director of the Master of Arts in Transformational Urban Leadership program at Azusa Pacific University, demonstrated on video: a neighborhood walk conducted as an act of community exegesis. The term is deliberate. Just as biblical exegesis involves careful, attentive reading of a text to understand what it is actually saying — rather than what we assume it says — community exegesis involves careful, attentive reading of a neighborhood to discern what is actually present and what God is already doing there.

The "Entering the Community" reading from your Second Local Workshop identified six dimensions of community exegesis:

  1. Structures — What kinds of buildings predominate? What does their condition and use reveal about investment, ownership, and change over time?
  2. "Scraps of life" — What artifacts do people leave around their property? What do local businesses stock and advertise? What do spending patterns reveal about who lives here?
  3. Signage — What do billboards sell, and in what language? What does graffiti communicate — gang territory or neighborhood art? What religious or political perspectives appear?
  4. Space — How is space defined and used? Who claims the front yard, the corner, the park? What do land-use patterns reveal about power and belonging?
  5. Sounds and smells — What music plays? What languages are spoken? What aromas suggest the cultural life of the neighborhood?
  6. Signs of hope — Where are God's people already at work? What churches, parachurch organizations, and community initiatives represent potential partners and existing capacity?

Virginia Ward's account of walking Pittsburgh neighborhoods with a group of youth leaders illustrates how community exegesis works in practice. While they were looking at an old church building, a man joined them and began offering a timeline of the neighborhood's history. He turned out to be the pastor. An elderly woman, watching from her window, followed his lead and filled in further details. These were the "key stakeholders" — not elected officials, but people whose knowledge of the neighborhood was deep, particular, and irreplaceable. "People are more than willing to tell you what is going on," Ward observed, "if you are willing to listen" (Ward, 2007).

This points to a crucial spiritual dimension of ABCD that Russell (2018) emphasized in his retrospective review of the framework's twenty-five years: ABCD is not primarily a technique but a posture. It requires the kind of "downward mobility" that Lupton described in the Walker Avenue context — a willingness to move slowly, to be present without an agenda, to receive the gifts of a neighborhood before offering your own. This is the posture of the incarnation: God entering the neighborhood, not parachuting in with a program, but "becoming flesh and dwelling among us" (John 1:14). When a ministry leader conducts a neighborhood asset walk in this spirit, they are not performing a social science exercise; they are practicing a form of incarnational discipleship.

Think About It

As you prepare for your neighborhood asset walk before Session 11, consider this question from the DVULI Second Local Workshop materials: "What would it mean for young people to be an authentic following of Jesus Christ who know the Lord, can articulate their faith, and are committed to being salt and light in their community?" How does ABCD create conditions in which that kind of faith can be lived, rather than merely professed?

ABCD and the DVULI Core Values: A Convergence

It is worth pausing to notice how completely ABCD maps onto the DVULI Core Values introduced in your First National Conference. Interdependence is the theological and practical foundation of ABCD — the conviction that community members are not isolated individuals but members of an organic web of gifts and relationships that, when activated, generate outcomes no individual could produce alone. This is precisely what Paul described in 1 Corinthians 12, and what the DVULI workbook summarized as "dancing solo — our failure to live in community" being the core problem that interdependence addresses.

Empowerment — creating "leader-full" organizations by releasing the potential of others — is what moves a ministry from betterment to development. The Empowerment Core Value's central question, "Do my staff and volunteers serve me, or do I serve them?", applies equally at the community level: "Does this program serve our organization's mission, or does it serve the community's own vision for itself?" The process of identifying, equipping, mobilizing, and supporting community members as leaders is the ABCD methodology in miniature.

Leverage — the process by which seemingly small actions produce big effects — is perhaps most clearly illustrated by the ABCD insight that the most powerful lever in community transformation is not money or external programs but the activation of existing capacity. A skills bank created from a neighborhood capacity inventory, connecting residents with the skills others need, can generate economic activity and mutual support at negligible cost. A single conversation between two residents who discover shared concerns can spark a collaboration that no grant application could have engineered. This is Leverage in its most biblical sense: the mustard seed principle, where small acts of faithful investment in existing gifts produce disproportionate Kingdom impact.

Your Neighborhood Asset Walk: Preparation for Session 11

This session concludes with a practical assignment that is central to the DVULI Second Local Workshop tradition: conducting a neighborhood asset walk with your youth before the next gathering. This is not a theoretical exercise but an embodied practice of community exegesis — what Michael Mata demonstrated in the video your cohort watched, walking and reading his Los Angeles neighborhood with fresh eyes.

The following guidelines will prepare you well:

  • Choose a specific geographic area — ideally a neighborhood central to your ministry or where most of your young people live. It should be small enough to walk (not drive or bike) and large enough to represent a variety of assets.
  • Bring your youth — this is essential. The asset walk is not research you conduct about young people; it is an exercise you conduct with them. Their capacity to see assets you would miss is itself a community asset you are developing.
  • Take photographs — document the assets you discover, including a group selfie. These images will ground the Session 11 debrief.
  • Use the Neighborhood Asset Map categories from your Second Local Workshop materials as a guide — but remain open to assets that fall outside any predetermined category. The unexpected find is often the most important one.
  • Read the neighborhood through all six exegetical lenses — structures, scraps of life, signage, space, sounds and smells, and especially signs of hope.
  • Ask your youth what they see as assets and why. Record their perceptions specifically. You will likely be surprised by what they notice that you miss.

After the walk, spend time in prayer and reflection before writing up your observations. Ask: What did God reveal about this neighborhood that I had not seen before? What assets did I encounter that represent potential leverage points for ministry? Who are the key stakeholders — the pastors, the porch-sitters, the longtime residents — whose knowledge of this community is a resource in itself? What would shift in your ministry strategy if you built from these assets rather than from a catalog of needs?

Key Takeaways

  • The Needs Map is accurate but incomplete — and its monopoly in community development funding actively disempowers the communities it claims to serve. Asset-Based Community Development offers a paradigm shift, not a repudiation of need, but an insistence that community transformation begins with gifts, not deficits.
  • Kretzmann and McKnight's three rings — Individual Assets, Citizens' Associations, and Local Institutions — provide a comprehensive framework for mapping every community's existing capacity. Every neighborhood, however distressed, contains vastly more than any Needs Map reveals.
  • The distinction between betterment and development is not about whether activities are good, but about whether they build or undermine community agency. The critical question is always: Are we doing things for the community, or are we working with and as the community?
  • Community exegesis — reading the story of a neighborhood through its structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope — is an act of incarnational discipleship, not merely a social research technique. It requires the posture of the learner, not the expert.
  • ABCD aligns deeply with the DVULI Core Values of Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage. Asset mapping is not simply good community development practice; it is the embodiment of these values in neighborhood ministry.
  • Virginia Ward's five-step process (Mapping → Building Relationships → Mobilizing → Convening → Leveraging Outside Resources) demonstrates that ABCD is a ministry practice, not an academic theory — and that it takes time: her community map took fifteen months to complete.
  • Your neighborhood asset walk with youth before Session 11 is not optional homework — it is the primary learning activity of this session, and the debriefing of your findings will be the heart of the next gathering.

Looking Ahead — Session 11

Session 11 will debrief your neighborhood asset walks and move from mapping to mobilizing — exploring how ABCD principles translate into concrete ministry strategy shifts, partnership development, and the creation of community-led initiatives that are genuinely rooted in what God has already placed in the neighborhood. Bring your photographs, your notes from the walk, and your youth's reflections. The richest material for our next conversation will come not from this reading but from what you encountered in the streets.

concepts: Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD): Individual Assets, Citizens' Associations, Local Institutions — the three rings of community capacity
Needs Map vs. Assets Map: the paradigm shift from deficit-focused to capacity-focused community engagement
Community exegesis: reading the story of a neighborhood through structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope
Betterment vs. Development: the critical distinction between doing things FOR a community and doing things WITH and AS a community

items.question: What is the fundamental paradigm shift that Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD) asks community leaders to make?
items.correctAnswer: Moving from mapping a community's deficits and needs to identifying and mobilizing its existing strengths and capacities
items.correctFeedback: ABCD's core paradigm shift is from a needs-map to an assets-map. Rather than cataloging problems like crime rates or dropout statistics, ABCD begins by identifying the gifts, skills, associations, and institutions already present in a community — and then mobilizing those capacities to generate transformation from the inside out.

distractors.text: Moving from secular community organizing strategies to explicitly faith-based ministry models
distractors.feedback: While ABCD is contextualized through a Christian ministry lens in this course, the paradigm shift Kretzmann and McKnight describe is not about adopting religious methods. It is about shifting from deficit-focused to capacity-focused thinking in how one sees and engages a community.

distractors.text: Moving from reliance on local volunteers to securing professional expertise from outside the community
distractors.feedback: This answer actually reverses the direction of the ABCD shift. ABCD moves away from dependence on outside expertise and toward recognizing and mobilizing the capacities of the people who already live in the community.

distractors.text: Moving from small neighborhood projects to large-scale systemic policy reform
distractors.feedback: ABCD is not primarily about scaling up to policy-level work. Its distinctive contribution is reorienting the starting point of community engagement — beginning with what is strong rather than what is wrong — regardless of the scale of the project.

items.question: According to the ABCD framework, which of the following is an example of the second ring of community capacity — Citizens' Associations?
items.correctAnswer: A neighborhood athletic league that brings together residents around shared interests
items.correctFeedback: Citizens' Associations are local organizations — churches, civic clubs, athletic leagues, cultural groups — that aggregate individual gifts and multiply their impact. An athletic league is a classic example because it gathers individual residents' passions and skills into a collective body that builds social capital.

distractors.text: A retired teacher's skill in tutoring children in reading
distractors.feedback: A retired teacher's tutoring skill is an Individual Asset — the first ring of community capacity. It represents a personal skill, knowledge, or capacity of an individual resident, not an organized group that aggregates multiple people's gifts.

distractors.text: The local public library's meeting rooms and book collection
distractors.feedback: A public library is a Local Institution — the third ring of community capacity. Institutions like libraries, hospitals, schools, and police departments differ from citizens' associations because they are formal, often publicly funded entities rather than voluntary organizations of residents.

distractors.text: A federal grant awarded to address neighborhood unemployment
distractors.feedback: An outside grant is an external resource, not one of the three internal rings of community capacity. In the ABCD five-step process, leveraging outside resources comes last — after internal assets have already been mapped, relationships built, and the community mobilized.

items.question: Kretzmann and McKnight argue that dominant nonprofit funding mechanisms actually reinforce deficit-based thinking. Why?
items.correctAnswer: Because they reward organizations for cataloging problems like teen pregnancy rates and crime statistics rather than documenting community capacities
items.correctFeedback: Kretzmann's critique is that the funding structures of the nonprofit sector require organizations to prove how bad conditions are in order to receive support. This creates a perverse incentive to emphasize deficits — teen pregnancy rates, crime statistics, dropout rates — which trains leaders to see communities as collections of problems rather than reservoirs of gifts.

distractors.text: Because they require organizations to hire only credentialed professionals from outside the community
distractors.feedback: While some funding mechanisms do favor professional credentials, Kretzmann's specific critique targets the way funding applications and reports are structured around deficit data. The core issue is the incentive to catalog problems, not hiring practices per se.

distractors.text: Because they distribute funding equally across all neighborhoods regardless of actual need
distractors.feedback: This misidentifies the problem. Kretzmann's argument is not about funding distribution formulas but about the way the entire system is designed around proving need through deficit statistics, which trains organizations and communities to define themselves by their problems.

distractors.text: Because they prioritize short-term emergency relief over long-term spiritual formation and community self-determination
distractors.feedback: While tensions between short-term and long-term work are real in ministry, this is not Kretzmann and McKnight's argument. Their specific critique is about how funding mechanisms reward deficit cataloging, shaping how organizations perceive and present their communities.

items.question: In the practice of community exegesis — 'reading' a neighborhood — which combination of observational categories does this session emphasize?
items.correctAnswer: Structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope
items.correctFeedback: Community exegesis involves carefully reading the story of a neighborhood through five observational lenses: structures (the built environment), sounds (what you hear), signage (posted messages and advertisements), space (how physical areas are used or neglected), and signs of hope (evidence of care, creativity, and community life). These categories train ministry leaders to pay attention with the same care they would bring to interpreting a biblical text.

distractors.text: Demographics, crime rates, income levels, education data, and housing values
distractors.feedback: These are all statistical indicators typically found on a needs map. Community exegesis is a qualitative, embodied practice of walking through a neighborhood and observing what is actually present — not analyzing census data or crime reports from a distance.

distractors.text: Churches, nonprofits, government agencies, schools, and businesses
distractors.feedback: While these are important institutions in ABCD's third ring of community capacity, they represent an institutional inventory rather than the sensory and spatial categories used in community exegesis. Community exegesis focuses on what you observe when you physically walk through a neighborhood.

distractors.text: Spiritual climate, pastoral leadership, worship attendance, small group participation, and giving patterns
distractors.feedback: These categories reflect an internal church audit, not community exegesis. Community exegesis reads the broader neighborhood — its physical environment, social dynamics, and signs of life — not just the health of a particular congregation.

items.question: What is the critical distinction between 'betterment' and 'development' as used in this session?
items.correctAnswer: Betterment is doing things FOR a community, while development is doing things WITH and AS a community
items.correctFeedback: This distinction is central to the session's challenge to ministry leaders. Betterment involves outsiders providing services or improvements to a community, which can create dependency and disempowerment. Development involves working alongside community members as co-participants, mobilizing the community's own assets so that transformation is generated from the inside out.

distractors.text: Betterment focuses on spiritual growth, while development focuses on economic progress
distractors.feedback: This answer imposes a sacred/secular divide that the session does not make. The betterment vs. development distinction is about the posture and process of engagement — whether outsiders act on behalf of a community or alongside it — not about whether goals are spiritual or material.

distractors.text: Betterment produces faster results, while development is slower but less expensive
distractors.feedback: While development may indeed take longer, the distinction is not about speed or cost. It is about who holds power and agency in the process — whether transformation is done to/for people or generated with and by people using their own capacities.

distractors.text: Betterment addresses symptoms of community problems, while development addresses their root systemic causes
distractors.feedback: The symptoms-vs.-root-causes framework is a valid concept in community work, but it is not the specific distinction this session draws. Betterment vs. development is specifically about relational posture: FOR (which creates dependency) versus WITH and AS (which honors community agency).

items.question: In the Kretzmann-McKnight five-step ABCD process, which step comes last?
items.correctFeedback: The five-step process is: (1) Mapping Community Assets, (2) Building Relationships, (3) Mobilizing for Economic Development, (4) Convening the Community, and (5) Leveraging Outside Resources. Placing outside resources last is a deliberate and countercultural design choice — it insists that a community must first discover and mobilize what it already has before seeking external support.

distractors.text: Mapping community assets
distractors.feedback: Mapping community assets is actually the first step in the process, not the last. It is the foundational activity that identifies the individual gifts, associations, and institutions present in the community before any mobilization begins.

distractors.text: Convening the community
distractors.feedback: Convening the community is the fourth step, not the fifth. It comes after assets have been mapped, relationships built, and economic development mobilized — but before outside resources are sought. The intentional ordering ensures the community has already organized itself internally.

distractors.text: Building relationships among asset holders
distractors.feedback: Building relationships is the second step in the process. It follows asset mapping because once you know what gifts exist, you need to connect people to one another. Placing this early reflects ABCD's conviction that relationships are the infrastructure through which assets become productive.

items.question: Dr. Robert Lupton's Walker Avenue case study in Atlanta is presented in the session primarily as an example of what principle?
items.correctAnswer: That authentic community requires joyful entanglement across race, class, and age rather than anonymity and self-sufficiency
items.correctFeedback: Lupton's Walker Avenue neighborhood demonstrates 'the Gospel in action' through what the session calls 'joyful entanglement' — deep, cross-boundary relationships that refuse the anonymity and self-sufficiency that make genuine community impossible. It serves as a portrait of what ABCD principles look like when incarnated in actual neighborhood life.

distractors.text: That well-funded external programs are the most effective means of neighborhood revitalization
distractors.feedback: This answer contradicts the core message of both the Walker Avenue case study and ABCD. Lupton's example is celebrated precisely because transformation came through resident relationships and mutual commitment, not through external program funding.

distractors.text: That homogeneous neighborhoods with shared socioeconomic backgrounds produce the strongest communities
distractors.feedback: Walker Avenue is presented as the opposite of homogeneity — its power lies in relationships across race, class, and age. The session uses this example to show that diversity of residents, not uniformity, can be a community asset when genuine relationship replaces isolation.

distractors.text: That a single visionary pastor can transform a neighborhood through strategic church planting and sustained pastoral presence
distractors.feedback: While Lupton is a significant leader, the Walker Avenue example is not about one person's heroic effort. It is about the collective, relational fabric of a whole neighborhood — an entanglement of many people's gifts and presence that constitutes community.

items.question: A ministry leader conducts an asset walk and discovers that several retired residents on one block are skilled gardeners, a local church has an unused half-acre lot, and the neighborhood elementary school wants to add nutrition education to its curriculum. According to ABCD, what should the leader do next with this information?
items.correctAnswer: Build relationships that connect the gardeners, the church, and the school so they can collaborate using their combined assets
items.correctFeedback: This scenario illustrates the ABCD process in action. After mapping assets (the gardeners' skills, the church's land, the school's curricular interest), the next step is building relationships among these asset holders. The leader's role is to be a connector — helping the community see how its own internal gifts can be woven together for mutual benefit without needing to seek outside help first.

distractors.text: Write a grant proposal to a foundation to fund a community garden program on the church lot
distractors.feedback: Seeking outside funding is the last step in the ABCD process, not the immediate next step. Jumping to grant writing before connecting internal assets reinforces the deficit-based model by implying the community cannot act without external money.

distractors.text: Compile the asset data into a report and present it to city officials for inclusion in urban planning documents
distractors.feedback: While informing city officials could be useful later, ABCD prioritizes internal community mobilization before engaging outside institutions. Handing data to external authorities bypasses the community's own agency and reduces residents to data points rather than active participants.

distractors.text: Hire a professional urban farmer to design and manage a garden program for the neighborhood
distractors.feedback: Bringing in an outside professional when skilled gardeners already live in the neighborhood exemplifies 'betterment' rather than 'development.' It does things FOR the community instead of WITH the community, overlooking the individual assets already identified on the asset walk.

items.question: Why does the ABCD framework describe local institutions such as hospitals, libraries, and police departments as 'treasure chests' of resources?
items.correctAnswer: Because when these institutions are reoriented toward community building, their existing staff, facilities, and budgets become accessible assets for residents
items.correctFeedback: The 'treasure chests' metaphor communicates that local institutions already contain enormous resources — personnel, physical space, equipment, expertise, and budgets — that are potentially available to the community. The key insight is that these resources exist regardless of new funding; what changes is whether the institution sees its mission as serving alongside the community rather than operating in isolation from it.

distractors.text: Because these institutions receive government funding that can be redirected to community organizations through formal grant applications
distractors.feedback: This answer reduces the 'treasure chest' metaphor to a funding transfer mechanism. ABCD's point is broader — institutions hold relational, spatial, and human resources, not just money. The metaphor is about reorienting institutional mission, not redirecting line items in a budget.

distractors.text: Because institutions are more reliable than individual residents, who may lack the commitment to sustain long-term community projects
distractors.feedback: This answer devalues individual assets — the first ring of community capacity — which ABCD treats as foundational. The framework does not rank institutions above individuals; rather, it sees all three rings as interdependent and necessary.

distractors.text: Because institutions have the legal authority to enforce community standards and hold residents accountable for participation
distractors.feedback: ABCD is built on voluntary participation and mutual empowerment, not enforcement or compulsion. Describing institutions as 'treasure chests' highlights their resources as gifts to be shared, not their authority to coerce community involvement.

items.question: A church has been operating a food pantry for ten years, providing groceries to 200 families weekly. The pastor learns about ABCD and wants to shift the ministry's approach. Which change would best reflect a genuine move from a needs-map to an assets-map orientation?
items.correctAnswer: Surveying pantry recipients to discover their skills and passions, then creating opportunities for them to contribute those gifts to the broader community
items.correctFeedback: This response embodies the full ABCD paradigm shift. It stops defining the 200 families solely by their need for food and begins treating them as people with gifts, skills, and capacities. By identifying and mobilizing those assets, the church moves from doing something FOR people (betterment) to creating a context where people participate WITH and AS community (development). This is the heart of seeing community as gift, not problem.

distractors.text: Increasing the pantry's efficiency by securing a larger facility and more food donations from corporate sponsors
distractors.feedback: Scaling up the existing model makes the pantry more effective at service delivery, but it does not change the underlying paradigm. Recipients are still defined by their need, and the church is still doing something FOR them. This is improved betterment, not a shift to development.

distractors.text: Replacing the food pantry with a job training program that addresses the root cause of food insecurity
distractors.feedback: While job training addresses a deeper issue, simply substituting one service for another does not constitute the ABCD shift. If the church designs and delivers the program for recipients who remain passive consumers, the relational dynamic is still deficit-based — it just targets a different deficit.

distractors.text: Adding a Bible study requirement to the food distribution so that the ministry addresses spiritual needs alongside physical needs
distractors.feedback: Adding a spiritual component does not change the needs-map orientation — it adds a spiritual need to the physical one. Both are still deficits the church is addressing FOR people. The ABCD shift requires seeing people as contributors with assets, not as recipients with multiple categories of need.

questions.stem: The Kretzmann-McKnight ABCD framework identifies three concentric rings of community assets. Which of the following correctly names all three?
options: Individual Assets, Citizens' Associations, Local Institutions
Financial Resources, Political Networks, Social Services
Personal Skills, Church Programs, Government Agencies
Community Needs, Service Providers, Funding Sources

feedback.correct: The three rings move from the most local and personal (individual gifts, skills, and capacities) outward to voluntary associations (churches, clubs, leagues) and then to formal institutions (schools, hospitals, businesses). Each ring represents assets already present in the community.
feedback.incorrect: A common mistake is to think of community capacity in terms of external or institutional resources. The ABCD framework deliberately begins with the gifts of individual residents, then moves to citizens' associations, and finally to local institutions — all of which are already inside the community.

variants.stem: In the ABCD framework, which ring of community assets refers to churches, civic clubs, athletic leagues, and cultural groups that aggregate individual gifts?
options: Citizens' Associations
Individual Assets
Local Institutions
External Partners

feedback.correct: Citizens' Associations occupy the middle ring of the ABCD framework. These voluntary organizations — churches, civic clubs, athletic leagues, cultural groups — serve as multipliers, aggregating the gifts of individuals and amplifying their impact beyond what any single person could achieve alone.
feedback.incorrect: Citizens' Associations are distinct from both Individual Assets (the innermost ring of personal skills, knowledge, and passions) and Local Institutions (the outer ring of schools, hospitals, police, and businesses). They are voluntary, relationship-driven organizations that connect people around shared interests.

questions.stem: According to the ABCD framework, what is the fundamental problem with needs-based approaches to community engagement?
options: They train communities to catalog deficits and depend on external intervention rather than mobilizing internal capacity
They are too expensive for most nonprofit organizations to implement
They ignore the spiritual dimension of community life
They focus too heavily on individual relationships rather than systemic change

feedback.correct: Kretzmann and McKnight argue that the dominant nonprofit funding model rewards organizations for documenting deficits — crime rates, dropout rates, teen pregnancy statistics — which creates a deficit identity. This needs-map orientation makes communities consumers of services rather than producers of solutions, undermining the very capacity it claims to build.
feedback.incorrect: The critique of needs-based approaches is not about cost, spirituality, or relationships. It is about the paradigm itself: when communities are defined by what they lack, the inevitable conclusion is that salvation must come from outside. ABCD reverses this by beginning with what the community already has.

variants.stem: Kretzmann's claim that 'communities can only be built by focusing on the strengths and capacities of the citizens who call that community home' most directly challenges which common practice?
options: Grant applications that require organizations to document community deficits as justification for funding
Strategic planning processes that set measurable goals
Pastoral counseling that focuses on individual spiritual growth
Academic research methods that use qualitative interviews

feedback.correct: Kretzmann's claim directly confronts the dominant funding mechanism in the nonprofit sector, which rewards organizations for cataloging problems — teen pregnancy rates, crime statistics, school dropout rates — rather than mapping the capacities already present. This deficit-documentation cycle reinforces the needs-map paradigm.
feedback.incorrect: Kretzmann's challenge is aimed squarely at the incentive structure of the nonprofit sector. The problem is not goal-setting, counseling, or research methods in general, but the specific practice of defining communities by their deficits in order to attract external resources.

questions.stem: A suburban megachurch raises $75,000, hires professional contractors, and builds a new basketball court in a low-income neighborhood without consulting any local residents. According to the ABCD framework, this approach is best classified as:
options: Betterment — doing things FOR the community
Development — doing things WITH the community
Asset mapping — identifying community resources
Community exegesis — reading the neighborhood's story
justifications: Because the church acted on behalf of residents without engaging their gifts, associations, or participation, which provides a tangible improvement but bypasses community capacity
Because the church spent money from outside the neighborhood, and any external funding automatically constitutes betterment
Because basketball courts are recreational rather than essential, making this a lower priority than housing or food programs
Because the project was led by a church rather than a secular organization

feedback.correct: The critical distinction between betterment and development is not the quality of the result but the process. Betterment delivers improvements to a community; development builds capacity within a community. By bypassing local residents entirely, the megachurch denied them the opportunity to contribute their own gifts and strengthened the dependency dynamic that ABCD seeks to dismantle.
feedback.incorrect: The betterment vs. development distinction is not about money, project type, or who leads the effort. It is about whether the community's own assets — individual gifts, associations, institutions — are identified and mobilized in the process. Doing something FOR people, even something good, is fundamentally different from doing something WITH and AS a community.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify betterment but attribute it to the wrong factor — such as external funding source or project type. The reason betterment is problematic is specifically that it bypasses community participation and capacity, not that it involves outside money per se.

variants.stem: A denominational office sends a team of volunteers to paint and repair homes in a rural community over a single weekend, selecting the homes themselves based on visible need. The local residents watch from their porches. According to ABCD, this is best classified as:
options: Betterment — doing things FOR the community
Development — doing things WITH the community
Community exegesis — reading the community's story
Asset mobilization — leveraging local institutions
justifications: Because the volunteers acted on the community's behalf without engaging local residents' skills, relationships, or decision-making, delivering a service rather than building capacity
Because volunteer projects are always betterment regardless of how they are organized
Because painting houses is a physical improvement rather than a relational one
Because the denominational office is a religious organization rather than a community organization

feedback.correct: The image of residents watching from their porches while outsiders improve their homes captures the betterment dynamic precisely. The homes may look better, but no local capacity has been built, no relationships have been strengthened, and the community has been positioned as a recipient rather than an agent of its own transformation.
feedback.incorrect: The betterment classification is not about volunteer work being inherently problematic. The issue is the process: when outsiders select, decide, and execute without engaging local residents' gifts and participation, they are doing FOR rather than WITH — regardless of how well-intentioned the effort is.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly identify betterment but misattribute it to the type of activity (painting) or the organization (denominational). The real issue is the absence of local participation, agency, and asset mobilization in the process.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The Kretzmann-McKnight ABCD process places 'Leveraging Outside Resources' as its final fifth step.
questions.reason: Genuine community development requires that internal assets — individual gifts, associations, and institutions — be identified, connected, and mobilized before external resources are sought.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. The deliberate placement of outside resources at step five is not accidental — it reflects the ABCD conviction that communities must first discover and activate what they already have. When external resources arrive before internal assets are mapped and mobilized, they tend to create dependency rather than development.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is to treat the sequence as arbitrary or to assume that external resources can be introduced at any point. The entire ABCD framework is built on the premise that internal capacity must come first — the five-step order (Mapping → Building Relationships → Mobilizing for Economic Development → Convening → Leveraging Outside Resources) embodies the paradigm shift from deficit to asset orientation.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The dominant nonprofit funding model rewards organizations for documenting community deficits such as crime rates, dropout rates, and teen pregnancy statistics.
variants.reason: Needs-based approaches position communities as consumers of external services rather than producers of local solutions.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, but they describe two related yet logically independent critiques. The funding model's deficit-documentation requirement is a structural feature of the nonprofit sector. The consumer-producer dynamic is a sociological consequence of needs-based approaches. While both are part of Kretzmann and McKnight's critique, the second does not explain why funding models are structured the way they are — it describes a separate consequence of the same paradigm.
feedback.incorrect: Students often assume that because both statements critique needs-based approaches, one must explain the other. However, the funding structure (rewarding deficit documentation) exists for its own institutional reasons (accountability, measurability), while the consumer dynamic is a separate downstream effect. Both are true, but the reason does not causally explain the assertion.

questions.stem: A ministry leader walks through a neighborhood and notices a hand-painted mural celebrating the community's history on the side of a bodega, the sound of gospel music drifting from an open church window, and a community garden with a sign reading 'Planted by Block 7 Neighbors.' Which elements of community exegesis are MOST directly represented in these observations?
options: Signs of hope, sounds, and signage
Structures, space, and signs of hope
Only sounds and signage
Structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope equally

feedback.correct: The mural and community garden represent signs of hope — evidence that residents are investing in their neighborhood's identity and shared life. The gospel music is a sound that reveals the community's spiritual and cultural texture. The garden sign ('Planted by Block 7 Neighbors') is signage that communicates ownership and collective action. While structures and space are also part of community exegesis, they are not the primary dimensions observed in this scenario.
feedback.incorrect: Community exegesis involves five specific dimensions: structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope. Each observation should be matched to the dimension it most directly represents. A mural and community garden are signs of hope (evidence of investment and aspiration), music is a sound, and labeled markers are signage. Not every walk will foreground all five dimensions equally.

variants.stem: During a neighborhood asset walk, a ministry leader observes boarded-up storefronts alongside a newly renovated community center, hears children laughing in a playground, and sees a banner reading 'Annual Block Party — 25th Year!' Which elements of community exegesis are MOST prominently represented?
options: Structures, sounds, and signage
Signs of hope, space, and sounds
Only structures and signage
All five elements equally

feedback.correct: The boarded-up storefronts and renovated community center are structures — physical features that tell a story about the neighborhood's past and present. The children laughing is a sound that reveals life and activity. The block party banner is signage communicating sustained community tradition. While signs of hope could also be inferred, the most direct observations map to structures, sounds, and signage.
feedback.incorrect: Each element of community exegesis corresponds to a specific type of observation. Structures are physical buildings and their condition. Sounds are auditory cues about neighborhood life. Signage is written or visual communication. Space refers to how areas are used and who occupies them. Signs of hope are evidence of community investment and aspiration.

questions.stem: According to Week 7's discussion of empowerment, what is the correct sequence for developing leaders within a ministry context?
options: Identifying → Equipping → Mobilizing → Supporting
Supporting → Mobilizing → Equipping → Identifying
Equipping → Identifying → Supporting → Mobilizing
Mobilizing → Supporting → Identifying → Equipping

feedback.correct: The empowerment process follows a deliberate sequence: first Identifying people with gifts and potential, then Equipping them with skills and knowledge, then Mobilizing them into active service, and finally Supporting them with ongoing encouragement and resources. This sequence ensures that deployment follows preparation, not the reverse.
feedback.incorrect: The empowerment sequence is intentionally ordered. You cannot equip someone you haven't identified, mobilize someone you haven't equipped, or support someone you haven't mobilized. Each step depends on the previous one, reflecting the servant leadership principle that development is a process, not an event.

variants.stem: In Week 7's framework for building a 'leader-full' organization, what does the empowerment process begin with?
options: Identifying the gifts and capacities of potential leaders
Mobilizing people into immediate ministry action
Providing financial support for leadership training programs
Equipping people with standardized curriculum materials

feedback.correct: The empowerment process always begins with Identifying — recognizing the gifts, skills, and passions that people already possess. This mirrors the ABCD principle of starting with assets rather than needs. A leader who skips identification and jumps straight to equipping or mobilizing risks deploying people into roles that don't match their gifts.
feedback.incorrect: Empowerment does not begin with action, funding, or curriculum. It begins with the patient work of identification — seeing what God has already placed in people. This discovery-first approach is what distinguishes empowerment from mere task assignment.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Kenda Creasy Dean's concept of 'consequential faith' identifies creed, community, call, and hope as four marks of deeply rooted adolescent faith.
questions.reason: The 40 Developmental Assets framework demonstrates that youth with more assets are statistically less likely to engage in high-risk behaviors.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true but logically independent. Dean's consequential faith framework is a theological account of what makes faith 'sticky' for adolescents — it describes the internal content of robust faith. The 40 Developmental Assets framework is a research-based sociological model of what young people need to thrive. While both address youth flourishing, the Developmental Assets research does not explain why Dean identified those particular four marks of faith.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes assume that because both frameworks address youth development, one must explain the other. However, Dean's consequential faith framework comes from theological analysis of the National Study of Youth and Religion, while the Developmental Assets come from Search Institute's sociological research. They are complementary but causally independent claims.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The 40 Developmental Assets framework reframes youth from 'problems to be fixed' to 'people lacking assets that communities can provide.'
variants.reason: External assets are things communities provide for young people, while internal assets are qualities young people develop within themselves.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. The reframing from 'youth as problems' to 'youth as people lacking assets' works precisely because the framework distinguishes between external assets (what communities provide — support, empowerment, boundaries, constructive use of time) and internal assets (what youth develop — commitment to learning, positive values, social competencies, positive identity). This distinction makes the community responsible for its contribution rather than blaming the young person.
feedback.incorrect: The external/internal asset distinction is not incidental to the reframing — it is the mechanism by which the reframing operates. When we see youth as 'problems,' we locate the deficit inside the young person. When we see youth as 'lacking assets,' we recognize that many of those assets are external — things the community has failed to provide. The distinction directly enables the paradigm shift.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about Asset-Based Community Development as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: Individual assets include the skills, knowledge, and passions of community residents.
Local institutions such as schools and hospitals automatically function as community-building resources.
External resources should be the first resource mobilized in community development.
A needs map can be strategically useful for securing grant funding.
Citizens' associations include churches, civic clubs, athletic leagues, and cultural groups.
Betterment and development produce identical long-term outcomes for communities.
Community exegesis requires professional training in urban planning before it can be practiced.

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here involve understanding boundary cases. Local institutions are assets, but only when reoriented toward community building — Kretzmann calls them 'treasure chests' that must be opened intentionally. Needs maps are critiqued by ABCD but remain strategically useful in a funding environment that rewards deficit documentation. The framework is not naive about institutional realities; it simply refuses to let the needs map define the community's identity.
feedback.incorrect: The most common errors involve treating local institutions as automatic assets (they require intentional reorientation) and dismissing needs maps entirely (ABCD critiques the needs paradigm but acknowledges that funders often require deficit data). Also, community exegesis is designed as a practice any ministry leader can undertake — it requires attentiveness, not professional credentials.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about ABCD practice and community ministry as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: The ABCD process begins with mapping assets already present in the community.
Doing things FOR a community constitutes genuine community development.
A community with significant visible poverty may still be rich in relational and associational assets.
Outside resources are harmful to community development.
An asset walk reveals only positive features of a neighborhood.
Churches can function simultaneously as citizens' associations and local institutions.
The five-step ABCD process must be completed sequentially without any overlap between steps.

feedback.correct: The subtleties matter: outside resources are not inherently harmful but become harmful when they arrive before internal capacity has been mobilized, creating dependency. Churches can function as citizens' associations (voluntary membership organizations) and as local institutions (established entities with buildings, budgets, and staff) depending on context. Community exegesis reveals the full texture of a neighborhood — including struggle — not just its positive features.
feedback.incorrect: Common errors include viewing outside resources as categorically bad (ABCD says they should come last, not never), assuming asset walks only look for good news (they read the whole story, including difficulty), and treating the five steps as rigidly sequential rather than as a guiding logic.

questions.stem: The Kretzmann-McKnight ABCD framework outlines a specific number of sequential steps for community development, beginning with mapping assets and concluding with leveraging outside resources. How many distinct steps are in this process?

feedback.correct: The Kretzmann-McKnight process has exactly 5 steps: (1) Mapping Community Assets, (2) Building Relationships, (3) Mobilizing for Economic Development, (4) Convening the Community, and (5) Leveraging Outside Resources. The deliberate sequencing ensures that internal capacity is fully activated before external resources enter the picture.
feedback.incorrect: Students often overestimate the number of steps, perhaps confusing the process steps with the elements of community exegesis (also 5) or the multiple categories of assets. Others underestimate, collapsing the middle relational and economic steps. The five steps represent a complete arc from discovery through mobilization to external partnership.

variants.stem: Community exegesis involves reading a neighborhood's story through a specific number of observational dimensions (structures, sounds, signage, space, and signs of hope). How many distinct dimensions does this framework include?

feedback.correct: Community exegesis uses exactly 5 observational dimensions: structures (physical buildings and their condition), sounds (auditory cues about neighborhood life), signage (written and visual communication), space (how areas are used and by whom), and signs of hope (evidence of investment, aspiration, and community vitality). Together, these five lenses help ministry leaders read the full story of a place.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes confuse the five exegesis dimensions with the five steps of the ABCD process or the three rings of community assets. The exegesis dimensions are specifically observational categories — they describe what you pay attention to when walking through a neighborhood, not the steps you take to develop it.

questions.stem: In Week 7, the 'Action Hero problem' describes a specific leadership failure. Which of the following best captures this concept?
options: A leader's instinct to perform individually undermines the development of collective ministry capacity
A leader who takes too many risks damages the organization's stability
A leader who delegates too much loses control of the ministry's direction
A leader who avoids conflict fails to address problems within the team

feedback.correct: The Action Hero problem describes how leaders default to individual performance — solving problems themselves, stepping in to 'save the day' — rather than building the capacity of others. This instinct feels heroic but actually prevents the development of a leader-full organization and undermines the collaborative intelligence that diverse gifts in genuine community can produce.
feedback.incorrect: The Action Hero problem is not about risk-taking, delegation, or conflict avoidance. It is specifically about the tension between individual performance and collective capacity. The 'hero' does real, good work — but by doing it alone, they prevent others from growing and the community from developing its own strength.

variants.stem: Week 7 introduced the concept of 'collaborative intelligence.' What does this term describe?
options: The superior results produced when diverse gifts operate in genuine community, exceeding what any individual could achieve alone
A leadership strategy where the smartest person in the group makes all key decisions
The use of artificial intelligence tools to coordinate ministry programs
A conflict resolution technique that requires unanimous agreement before action

feedback.correct: Collaborative intelligence describes the phenomenon where diverse gifts, operating in genuine interdependent community, produce results that no single individual — however talented — could achieve alone. This concept is grounded in the 1 Corinthians 12 body metaphor, where each part contributes something irreplaceable to the whole.
feedback.incorrect: Collaborative intelligence is not about one person deciding for the group, nor about technology, nor about unanimous agreement. It is about the emergent capacity that arises when diverse people contribute their unique gifts in a context of genuine mutual dependence — the whole becomes greater than the sum of its parts.

questions.stem: Pastor Maria has recently moved into a neighborhood to begin a new ministry.
